nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich and rubble areas of lagoon and seaward reefs, typically at depths of 1–30 m.

diet.notes: Has been documented grazing on microalgae, detritus, small crustaceans, and zooplankton in the wild. In captivity, has been observed accepting spirulina, marine algae, high-quality angelfish preparations, mysis shrimp, and frozen brine shrimp. Providing a varied diet with regular algae supplement tends to support long-term health.

imageCandidates.notes: Francois Libert; 998x587. Sharp clean side profile best showing the diagnostic dusky-brown body with vivid electric-blue dorsal/anal fin margins and orange eye/mouth ring. Minor fingertip in top-left corner.
Diego Delso; 5146x3431. High-resolution whole-fish in-situ reef shot, blue eye ring, dark shoulder spot and blue fin margins visible; body slightly dark. Different individual for variety.

Elisabeth Morcel (via Philippe Bourjon); 1200x875. Clean lateral profile showing dark shoulder spot, faint vertical striations and bright blue anal-fin margin. Reunion individual, adds locality/angle variety.

summary: The Bluefin Angelfish is an Indo-Pacific dwarf angel generally regarded as intermediate in care, recognized by its iridescent blue-tinged fins and tendency to graze on algae and small invertebrates. It is generally advised to be kept singly, with plenty of live rock, in a well-established system.
careNotes:
The Bluefin Angelfish (Centropyge multispinis) is generally considered one of the hardier dwarf angelfish, though it tends to be semi-aggressive — particularly in smaller aquariums. It is generally advised to introduce this species last when stocking a tank, which tends to reduce territorial behavior toward new arrivals. Only one individual should typically be kept per system unless a confirmed mated pair is available.

This species is commonly considered reef-safe with caution. Isolated cases of nipping at soft and stony corals, clam mantles, feather dusters, and anemones have been reported, and well-fed individuals tend to exhibit less of this behavior. It is not an obligate corallivore, but a degree of risk to sessile invertebrates should be expected.

Ample live rock with crevices, overhangs, and caves is recommended to provide both grazing surfaces — where the fish will browse algae and detritus — and shelter. The species has been reported susceptible to common marine parasitic diseases such as marine ich (Cryptocaryon irritans) and velvet (Amyloodinium ocellatum), as well as secondary bacterial infections when stressed.

Like all Centropyge dwarf angelfish, wild Bluefin Angelfish are protogynous hermaphrodites, meaning individuals begin life as females and dominant individuals may transition to male. Captive sex change has been noted but has not been reliably induced under aquarium conditions.
breedingNotes: Has been reported as very difficult to breed in captivity. Wild populations are protogynous hermaphrodites (female-first sex changers), with no reliably distinguishable external differences between males and females in captive specimens. Captive breeding attempts are rarely documented in the hobby literature.

additional.name: BlueZoo Aquatics
additional.notes: Secondary source. Adult size given as 5.5 in (13.97 cm) vs LiveAquaria's 6 in (15.24 cm). Minimum tank size given as 30 gal (113.55 L) vs LiveAquaria's 70 gal (264.95 L) — significant disagreement flagged.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomy, native range, and maximum size (14.0 cm TL) cross-checked. Page returned 403; data sourced via WebSearch summary of FishBase records.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping
additional.notes: Cross-checked sizing (up to 6 in), temperature (72–78°F), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity (1.020–1.025), tank size (70 gal), protogynous hermaphroditism, lifespan (up to 15 years), and reef-safe-with-caution status. Agreed with LiveAquaria on most parameters.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been reported as very difficult to breed in captivity. Wild populations are protogynous hermaphrodites with no reliably distinguishable external sex differences. Documented captive breeding is rare.
